name: Naturepedia Carbon Cycle Knowledge Mesh
description: Paid relationship mesh connecting Carbon Cycle™, Soil Systems™, Soil Carbon Systems™, Forest Carbon Systems™, Carbon & Microbial Life™, Water Systems™, Plant Intelligence™, Ecosystem Feedbacks™, and Climate Carbon Feedbacks™.
architecture: Carbon Cycle → Soil Systems → Soil Carbon Systems → Carbon & Microbial Life → Forest Carbon Systems → Climate Carbon Feedbacks → Ecosystem Feedbacks → Earth Systems

relationshipMesh.relationship: Plants capture light and atmospheric carbon through photosynthesis, converting carbon dioxide into sugars that support plant growth, soil feeding, and ecosystem energy flow.
Photosynthesis is the biological entry point for atmospheric carbon into living systems, forests, roots, soils, microbial communities, and food webs.
Carbon enters soils through roots, litter, microbial biomass, fungal networks, organic matter, plant residues, and decomposing biological material.
Soil systems stabilize carbon through aggregates, clay-mineral interactions, root exudates, fungal pathways, microbial residues, moisture, and organic matter protection.
Microbial communities decompose, transform, respire, store, and stabilize carbon through soil metabolism, nutrient exchange, biomass turnover, and mineral association.
Decomposition converts dead organic matter into nutrients, carbon dioxide, microbial biomass, humus, and long-term soil organic matter pools.
Forests sequester carbon in trunks, branches, leaves, roots, deadwood, leaf litter, mycorrhizal networks, soil organic matter, and long-lived biomass.
Water regulates carbon movement through wetlands, rivers, floodplains, groundwater, estuaries, blue carbon systems, soil moisture, and plant productivity.
Carbon cycling influences atmospheric carbon dioxide, methane dynamics, warming feedbacks, ecosystem respiration, soil carbon loss, forest storage, and climate regulation.
Climate-carbon feedbacks alter forests, soils, water cycles, microbial activity, fire regimes, plant growth, decomposition rates, and ecosystem resilience.
Regenerative carbon systems strengthen Earth system resilience by restoring soil carbon, water retention, biodiversity, forest health, microbial function, and carbon storage.
